data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "Egypt survived because its ideological-theological structure allowed ritual delegation of military authority to generals without fracturing divine kingship, whereas Hittite and Mycenaean charismatic authority collapsed when kings personally failed in battle", "mechanism": "Variegated legitimation structures → differential resilience of central authority to military defeat → cascading administrative dissolution vs. hierarchical reconstitution", "scope": {"covers": ["Egyptian survival", "Hittite collapse", "Mycenaean collapse", "comparative political theology"], "excludes": ["Ugarit (merchant oligarchy, not kingship)", "Levantine city-states (heterogeneous political forms)", "material causes of military defeat"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Egyptian kingship also fragmented with Libyan and Nubian challengers establishing rival dynasties during same period", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Hittite kingship survived relocation to Syro-Hittite states with identical ideological structure intact", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Mycenaean wanax authority already weakened by 1300 BC with no recovery possible", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Directly challenges materialist explanations by centering political-theological variation; treats Egypt's 'diminished' survival as genuine institutional continuity requiring explanation; connects to skeptic school emphasis on counterexamples to monocausal materialism"}
